62ead0be8c91030f69d5b547.question: In the prologue to this play, one character who is searching for his daughters, in each city “seeks out all the courtesans...and prolongs the night in his enquiries”. That man is described as a bird by a slave due to his long tunic, who then calls him a “Gugga”. That slave eventually abuses that man as a “crawling reptile”, after a comic sequence in which (*) Milphio, the slave, “translates” this title character’s words into nonsense about bringing African mice for the games. Best known for the speech by Hanno in Punic, and whose title is a diminutive based on his home town, for 10 points, name this Latin comedy by Plautus.
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b547.question_sanitized: In the prologue to this play, one character who is searching for his daughters, in each city "seeks out all the courtesans...and prolongs the night in his enquiries". That man is described as a bird by a slave due to his long tunic, who then calls him a "Gugga". That slave eventually abuses that man as a "crawling reptile", after a comic sequence in which (*) Milphio, the slave, "translates" this title character's words into nonsense about bringing African mice for the games. Best known for the speech by Hanno in Punic, and whose title is a diminutive based on his home town, for 10 points, name this Latin comedy by Plautus.

62ead0be8c91030f69d5b55b.question: This author’s earliest published work described the opening of the Colosseum, while they also produced two sets of poems, the Xenia and the Apophoreta meant to accompany gifts during Saturnalia. In one poem about a fellow author, this poet wrote “Let all girls read (*) Sulpicia if they want to please their husband alone”. In this poet’s retirement, he wrote gloatingly that Juvenal might be “wandering restless in the noisy Suburra”, while in an address to the reader he claims that he his “known over the whole world for his humorous books”. Author of the line “my poems are naughty, but my life is pure”, for 10 points, name this prolific Flavian poet of epigrams.
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b55b.question_sanitized: This author's earliest published work described the opening of the Colosseum, while they also produced two sets of poems, the Xenia and the Apophoreta meant to accompany gifts during Saturnalia. In one poem about a fellow author, this poet wrote "Let all girls read (*) Sulpicia if they want to please their husband alone". In this poet's retirement, he wrote gloatingly that Juvenal might be "wandering restless in the noisy Suburra", while in an address to the reader he claims that he his "known over the whole world for his humorous books". Author of the line "my poems are naughty, but my life is pure", for 10 points, name this prolific Flavian poet of epigrams.

62ead0be8c91030f69d5b56c.question: Gail Trimble has suggested that “we might have invented the genre” this poem is categorized in simply because of this “important, widely read and perplexing Latin poem”. This poem addresses those “born in a chosen race”, and later laments “afterwards earth was tainted by impious wickedness”. The Three Fates sing a gloomy (*) epithalamium with the repeated line “fly, guiding threads, fly spindle” predicting the birth, life and death of Achilles, future child of the wedded couple. With an extended ekphrasis of the tale of Ariadne and Theseus, for 10 points, name this longest poem of 1st century BC neoteric poet, about the marriage of Peleus and Thetis.
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b56c.question_sanitized: Gail Trimble has suggested that "we might have invented the genre" this poem is categorized in simply because of this "important, widely read and perplexing Latin poem". This poem addresses those "born in a chosen race", and later laments "afterwards earth was tainted by impious wickedness". The Three Fates sing a gloomy (*) epithalamium with the repeated line "fly, guiding threads, fly spindle" predicting the birth, life and death of Achilles, future child of the wedded couple. With an extended ekphrasis of the tale of Ariadne and Theseus, for 10 points, name this longest poem of 1st century BC neoteric poet, about the marriage of Peleus and Thetis.
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b56c.answer: Catullus 64 or his epyllion (accept Marriage of Peleus and Thetis before the giveaway) [yes, that is 2009 University Challenge icon Dr Gail Trimble]
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b56c.answer_sanitized: Catullus 64 or his epyllion (accept Marriage of Peleus and Thetis before the giveaway) [yes, that is 2009 University Challenge icon Dr Gail Trimble]

62ead0be8c91030f69d5b580.question: This poet describes a man who “sips old wine, bottled when Consuls wore their hair long”, who orders “distilled water cooler than frost in Thrace”. That man,is served a lamprey from Messina, while the addressee is offered “the stringy snake’s relative” or “a denizen of that drain beneath the heart of (*) Subura”. This poet mocks that if you were given four thousand in gold, “you’d turn from a nobody into one of Virro’s dear friends”. This poet concludes that poem by declaring that “you won’t be afraid to endure the whip: that’s the dinner and friend you’re worthy of”. For 10 points, name this acerbic Roman, who describes the worst possible dinner party in his fifth satire.
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b580.question_sanitized: This poet describes a man who "sips old wine, bottled when Consuls wore their hair long", who orders "distilled water cooler than frost in Thrace". That man,is served a lamprey from Messina, while the addressee is offered "the stringy snake's relative" or "a denizen of that drain beneath the heart of (*) Subura". This poet mocks that if you were given four thousand in gold, "you'd turn from a nobody into one of Virro's dear friends". This poet concludes that poem by declaring that "you won't be afraid to endure the whip: that's the dinner and friend you're worthy of". For 10 points, name this acerbic Roman, who describes the worst possible dinner party in his fifth satire.

62ead0be8c91030f69d5b58f.question: This poet suggests we should place all our trust in the gods, “since fair winds swiftly follow harsh gales”. This poet is recorded as going into exile in Sicily on the Marmor Parium. The first possible reception of this poet is a fragment of Anacreon which laments that his hair is now white. In one poem this poet denies that “a fleet is the finest thing on the dark earth” (*) , but instead “it is whoever one loves”. A later comedy is probably the source of their supposed spouse, “Little Prick from the Isle of Man”. For 10 points, name this poet, who in a recently discovered poem discusses her brothers Charaxos and Larichos, who came from the island of Lesbos.
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b58f.question_sanitized: This poet suggests we should place all our trust in the gods, "since fair winds swiftly follow harsh gales". This poet is recorded as going into exile in Sicily on the Marmor Parium. The first possible reception of this poet is a fragment of Anacreon which laments that his hair is now white. In one poem this poet denies that "a fleet is the finest thing on the dark earth" (*) , but instead "it is whoever one loves". A later comedy is probably the source of their supposed spouse, "Little Prick from the Isle of Man". For 10 points, name this poet, who in a recently discovered poem discusses her brothers Charaxos and Larichos, who came from the island of Lesbos.

62ead0be8c91030f69d5b5a7.question: The scholia suggest that two of this set of poems were written for participants in the same event, a Rhodian and a Corinthian, and the survival of these poems is in part due to their usefulness in reconstructing chronology. Kathryn Morgan has recently published a book about the author of these poems and “the Construction of the Syracusan Monarchy”. The most famous of these poems, the first of one collection, which opens “water is best”, is in praise of (*) Hieron of Syracuse, and his victory in the single horse race. For 10 points, name these poems, composed by Pindar for victors at the most prestigious of the Panhellenic Games.
62ead0be8c91030f69d5b5a7.question_sanitized: The scholia suggest that two of this set of poems were written for participants in the same event, a Rhodian and a Corinthian, and the survival of these poems is in part due to their usefulness in reconstructing chronology. Kathryn Morgan has recently published a book about the author of these poems and "the Construction of the Syracusan Monarchy". The most famous of these poems, the first of one collection, which opens "water is best", is in praise of (*) Hieron of Syracuse, and his victory in the single horse race. For 10 points, name these poems, composed by Pindar for victors at the most prestigious of the Panhellenic Games.

62ead0be8c91030f69d5b5ce.question: Bacchus watches one act of this character, and asks them to repeat what they did for the nymphs of Mount Nysa who raised him. This character is described fleeing from the wrath of another to a place which knew Phene and Periphas, and how they bother flew ‘as birds, the eagle and the osprey’. This character later vanishes from that place ‘in a dark mist’ in their last appearance in that poem. Heroides 12, composed in the voice of this person, has been seen as metapoetic prequel to their appearance in a later poem, as this character declares that ‘something greater is stirring in my mind’. The subject of much of Book 7 of the (*) Metamorphoses, this character is introduced debating with herself, asking ‘I wonder if this, or something like this, is what people indeed call love. For 10 points, who asks ‘who but the heartless, would not be touched by Jason’s youth and birth and courage’.

This character later vanishes from that place 'in a dark mist' in their last appearance in that poem. Heroides 12, composed in the voice of this person, has been seen as metapoetic prequel to their appearance in a later poem, as this character declares that 'something greater is stirring in my mind'. The subject of much of Book 7 of the (*) Metamorphoses, this character is introduced debating with herself, asking 'I wonder if this, or something like this, is what people indeed call love. For 10 points, who asks 'who but the heartless, would not be touched by Jason's youth and birth and courage'.

62ead0be8c91030f69d5b5d1.question: TWO ANSWERS REQUIRED One of these two characters is handed a bag as they depart to give to ‘Cleophon and to the Tax collectors, Myrmex and Nichomachus’. The other of these two characters asks mockingly why the other talks of Parnassus and Lycabettus, in response to criticism that it is their duty to ‘conceal the rot’. A third character who might be expected to take part in a dispute between these two characters is reported to be happy to ‘sit on the fence’ but that he will challenge one of them ‘for the sake of (*) tragedy’ if they win. The choice of a deity between these two characters is made when they offer advice to a city that ‘they’ll save themselves when they consider the land of the enemy as their land and their land as that of the enemy’ For 10 points name these two characters, whose metatheatrical contest judged by Dionysus has a central role in The Frogs.
